passages.text: The universe (which others call the Library) is composed of an indefinite and perhaps infinite number of hexagonal galleries.
passages.context: Opening of the Library of Babel - the universe as infinite text

passages.text: The Library is a sphere whose exact center is any one of its hexagons and whose circumference is inaccessible.
passages.context: Every point is the center - spatial infinity

passages.text: The Library exists ab aeterno. No reasonable mind can doubt that fact. Man, that imperfect librarian, may be the work of chance or of malevolent demiurges; the universe, with its elegant endowment of shelves, of enigmatic volumes, of indefatigable stairs, can only be the work of a god.
passages.context: The library as divine creation

passages.text: There are no two identical books. From those incontrovertible premises, the librarian deduced that the Library is total—perfect, complete, and whole.
passages.context: The library contains all possible books

passages.text: Everything is there: the minute history of the future, the autobiographies of the archangels, the faithful catalog of the Library, thousands and thousands of false catalogs.
passages.context: The library contains all truths and all lies

passages.text: The certitude that everything has already been written annuls us, or renders us phantasmal.
passages.context: If all is written, what remains for us?

passages.text: Perhaps my old age and fearfulness deceive me, but I suspect that the human species—the unique species—is about to be extinguished, but the Library will endure: illuminated, solitary, infinite, perfectly motionless, equipped with precious volumes, useless, incorruptible, secret.
passages.context: The library outlasts humanity

passages.text: Mirrors and fatherhood are abominable because they multiply and extend the visible universe.
passages.context: The heresiarchs of Uqbar's doctrine

passages.text: For the people of Tlön, the world is not an amalgam of objects in space; it is a heterogeneous series of independent acts—the world is successive, temporal, but not spatial.
passages.context: Tlön's idealist philosophy

passages.text: The metaphysicians of Tlön seek not truth, or even plausibility—they seek to amaze, astound. In their view, metaphysics is a branch of the literature of fantasy.
passages.context: Philosophy as creative fiction

passages.text: How could I not submit to Tlön, to the minute and vast evidence of an orderly planet? It is useless to answer that reality is also orderly. Perhaps it is, but in accordance with divine laws which we will never quite grasp.
passages.context: The fictional world becomes more convincing than reality

passages.text: The world will be Tlön.
passages.context: Fiction overtakes reality

passages.text: No one saw him disembark in the unanimous night. No one saw the bamboo canoe sinking into the sacred mud. But within a few days no one was unaware that the taciturn man had come from the South.
passages.context: The arrival of the dreamer

passages.text: He wanted to dream a man: he wanted to dream him with minute integrity and insert him into reality.
passages.context: The central task - dreaming a person into existence

passages.text: In the cosmogonies of the Gnostics, the demiurges knead and mold a red Adam who cannot stand on his feet; as inept and crude and elementary as that Adam of dust was the Adam of dream fabricated by the nights of the wizard.
passages.context: Early failures in dreaming a man

passages.text: With relief, with humiliation, with terror, he understood that he too was a mere appearance, dreamt by another.
passages.context: The dreamer discovers he too is dreamed - the famous ending

passages.text: Time forks perpetually toward innumerable futures. In one of them I am your enemy.
passages.context: The nature of forking time

passages.text: The Garden of Forking Paths is an incomplete but not false image of the universe as conceived by Ts'ui Pên. Unlike Newton and Schopenhauer, your ancestor did not believe in a uniform and absolute time. He believed in an infinite series of times, in a dizzily growing, ever spreading network of diverging, converging and parallel times.
passages.context: The revelation of what the garden represents

passages.text: This web of time—the strands of which approach one another, bifurcate, intersect, or ignore each other through the centuries—embraces every possibility. We do not exist in most of them. In some you exist and not I, while in others I do, and you do not.
passages.context: The web of all possible times

passages.text: I come now to the ineffable center of my tale; it is here that a writer's hopelessness begins. Every language is an alphabet of symbols the employment of which assumes a past shared by its interlocutors. How can one transmit to others the infinite Aleph, which my timorous memory can scarcely contain?
passages.context: The impossibility of describing the Aleph

passages.text: I saw the Aleph from everywhere at once, saw the earth in the Aleph, and the Aleph once more in the earth and the earth in the Aleph, saw my face and my viscera, saw your face, and I felt dizzy, and I wept, because my eyes had seen that secret, hypothetical object whose name has been usurped by men but which no man has ever truly looked upon: the inconceivable universe.
passages.context: Seeing everything simultaneously

passages.text: The Aleph was probably two or three centimeters in diameter, but universal space was contained inside it, with no diminution in size. Each thing was infinite things, because I could clearly see it from every point in the cosmos.
passages.context: The Aleph contains everything

passages.text: The other one, the one called Borges, is the one things happen to. I know of Borges from the mail and see his name on a list of professors or in a biographical dictionary.
passages.context: The split between the public and private self

passages.text: I do not know which of us has written this page.
passages.context: The final line - who is the author?

passages.text: A man sets out to draw the world. As the years go by, he peoples a space with images of provinces, kingdoms, mountains, bays, ships, islands, fishes, rooms, instruments, stars, horses, and individuals. A short time before he dies, he discovers that the patient labyrinth of lines traces the lineaments of his own face.
passages.context: All creation is self-portrait

passages.text: Being with you and not being with you is the only way I have to measure time.
passages.context: Love as the measure of time

passages.text: I have always imagined that Paradise will be a kind of library.
passages.context: Borges's vision of eternity

passages.text: To fall in love is to create a religion that has a fallible god.
passages.context: The nature of romantic love

passages.text: Reality is not always probable, or likely.
passages.context: On the implausibility of the real

passages.text: We accept reality so readily—perhaps because we sense that nothing is real.
passages.context: Our easy acceptance of the world

passages.text: The original is unfaithful to the translation.
passages.context: The paradox of translation

passages.text: Any life, however long and complicated it may be, actually consists of a single moment—the moment when a man knows forever more who he is.
passages.source: A Biography of Tadeo Isidoro Cruz
passages.context: Life reduced to one defining moment

passages.text: God moves the player, and he, the piece. What god behind God originates the scheme of dust and time and dream and agony?
passages.context: The infinite regress of causation

passages.text: The truth is that we live out our lives putting off all that can be put off; perhaps we all know deep down that we are immortal and that sooner or later all men will do and know all things.
passages.context: On human procrastination

passages.text: He was, let us not forget, almost incapable of general, platonic ideas. Not only was it difficult for him to see that the generic symbol 'dog' took in all the dissimilar individuals of all shapes and sizes, it irritated him that the 'dog' of three-fourteen in the afternoon, seen in profile, should be indicated by the same noun as the dog of three-fifteen, seen from the front.
passages.context: Funes's inability to generalize

passages.text: The steps a man takes from the day of his birth until that of his death trace in time an inconceivable figure. The Divine Mind intuitively grasps that form immediately, as men do a triangle.
passages.context: Life as a shape visible only to God

passages.text: Whosoever would undertake some atrocious enterprise should act as if it were already accomplished, should impose upon himself a future as irrevocable as the past.
passages.context: How to commit to difficult actions

passages.text: There is a concept that is the corruptor and destroyer of all others. I speak not of Evil, whose limited empire is that of ethics; I speak of the infinite.
passages.context: The dangerous concept of infinity

passages.text: Writing is nothing more than a guided dream.
passages.context: The nature of literary creation

passages.text: There is no intellectual exercise that is not ultimately pointless. A philosophical doctrine is, at first, a plausible description of the universe; the years go by, and it is a mere chapter—if not a paragraph or a noun—in the history of philosophy.
passages.source: Pierre Menard, Author of the Quixote
passages.context: The fate of all intellectual systems

passages.text: He wanted to dream a man. He wanted to dream him completely, in painstaking detail, and impose him upon reality.
passages.context: The story's central premise: imagination as an act of absolute creative will, the dreamer as demiurge who wills a person into existence.

passages.text: He understood that the task of molding the incoherent and dizzying stuff that dreams are made of is the most difficult work a man can undertake, even if he fathom all the enigmas of the higher and lower orders—much more difficult than weaving a rope of sand or minting coins of the faceless wind.
passages.context: Borges names dreaming into form as the hardest of all labors—harder than any physical or intellectual feat, because the material itself resists coherence.

passages.text: He dreamed the heart warm, active, secret—about the size of a closed fist, a garnet-colored thing inside the dimness of a human body that was still faceless and sexless; he dreamed it, with painstaking love, for fourteen brilliant nights.
passages.context: The dreamer builds his created man organ by organ, beginning with the heart—the center of life dreamed before the face is known, love before identity.

passages.text: With relief, with humiliation, with terror, he realized that he, too, was but appearance, that another man was dreaming him.
passages.context: The story's devastating reversal: the creator discovers he is himself a creation, dreamed by another. All certainty of independent existence dissolves.

passages.text: Each part of the house occurs many times; any particular place is another place. There is no one pool, courtyard, drinking trough, manger; the mangers, drinking troughs, courtyards, pools are fourteen (infinite) in number.
passages.context: The Minotaur's first-person description of the labyrinth as a place where every element is both itself and all its instances—structure as ontology, architecture as identity.

passages.text: The house is as big as the world—or rather, it is the world.
passages.context: The labyrinth and the world are identical to the one who has never left. Confinement indistinguishable from cosmos—the prisoner who knows no outside has no inside either.

passages.text: I like to pretend that there is another Asterion, and that I am showing him around the house. With great courtesy I say to him: Now we will return to the intersection before, or: Now we will go down this stairway.
passages.context: The Minotaur invents a companion-double to relieve his solitude—imagination as the only form of relationship available to the utterly isolated, the self doubled to create society.

passages.text: Every nine years, nine men enter the house so that I can free them from all evil. I hear their steps or their voices in the depths of the stone galleries and I run joyfully to find them... I do not know who they are, but I know that one of them prophesied, at the moment of his death, that someday my redeemer would come.
passages.context: The Minotaur recounts the ritual murders as acts of liberation—his violence understood by him as mercy. Monstrousness and innocence occupying the same being simultaneously.

passages.text: I come now to the ineffable center of my tale; it is here that a writer's hopelessness begins. Every language is an alphabet of symbols the employment of which assumes a past shared by its interlocutors. How can one transmit to others the infinite Aleph, which my timorous memory can scarcely contain?
passages.context: Borges names the fundamental impossibility at the heart of mystical writing: language is sequential, the vision was simultaneous, and no chain of words can hold what was seen all at once.

passages.text: In that unbounded moment, I saw millions of delightful and horrible acts; none amazed me so much as the fact that all occupied the same point, without superposition and without transparency. What my eyes saw was simultaneous; what I shall write is successive, because language is successive.
passages.context: The Aleph reveals language's fundamental structural lie: it must be read in time, yet the truth it tries to tell was apprehended outside of time. The gap between vision and report is unbridgeable.

passages.text: I saw the populous sea, saw dawn and dusk, saw the multitudes of the Americas, saw a silvery spiderweb at the center of a black pyramid, saw a broken labyrinth (it was London), saw endless eyes, all very close, studying themselves in me as though in a mirror, saw all the mirrors on the planet (and none of them reflecting me).
passages.context: The catalogue of simultaneous visions—an ekphrasis of everything—culminates in the image of all mirrors reflecting everything except the seer himself. To see all is to disappear from what is seen.

passages.text: I saw my face and my viscera, saw your face, and I felt dizzy, and I wept, because my eyes had seen that secret, hypothetical object whose name has been usurped by men but which no man has ever truly looked upon: the inconceivable universe.
passages.context: The culmination of the Aleph vision—seeing oneself from outside, seeing the reader, then weeping. Total vision is also total vulnerability. The universe was always there; what is unbearable is finally seeing it.

passages.text: The place where, without admixture or confusion, all the places of the world, seen from every angle, coexist.
passages.context: The technical definition of the Aleph: not a metaphor but a literal point containing all spatial perspectives simultaneously. Multiplicity without confusion, everything without mixture.

passages.text: He had never lingered among the pleasures of memory. Impressions, momentary and vivid, would wash over him: a potter's vermilion glaze; the sky-vault filled with stars that were also gods; the moon, from which a lion had fallen; the smoothness of marble under his sensitive, slow fingertips.
passages.context: Homer before blindness: the poet lived entirely in sensory flashes, absorbing the world without organizing it—a pure receptivity that was itself the raw material of epic.

passages.text: Gradually, the splendid universe began drawing away from him; a stubborn fog blurred the lines of his hand; the night lost its peopling stars, the earth became uncertain under his feet. Everything grew distant, and indistinct. When he learned that he was going blind, he cried out.
passages.context: The loss of the visual world is felt as abandonment—the universe pulling back. Blindness is not just a medical fact but a cosmological event: the world itself receding.

passages.text: In this night of his mortal eyes into which he was now descending, love and adventure were also awaiting him. Ares and Aphrodite—because now he began to sense a rumor of glory and hexameters, a rumor of men who defend a temple that the gods will not save... the rumor of the Odysseys and Iliads that it was his fate to sing and to leave echoing in the cupped hands of human memory.
passages.context: The descent into blindness becomes the ascent into creative vision. What the mortal eyes lose, the inner ear gains—Homer's epics were composed in the dark, which is why they can still be heard.

passages.text: He felt, inexplicably, the way one might feel upon recognizing a melody or a voice, that all this had happened to him before and that he had faced it with fear but also with joy and hopefulness and curiosity.
passages.context: In the moment of blindness's onset, Homer feels a paradoxical recognition—this darkness is familiar, this descent has a form. The great loss arrives already known.

passages.text: In my childhood I was a fervent worshiper of the tiger... In that underground sea or chaos, they still endure. As I sleep I am drawn into some dream or other, and suddenly I realize that it's a dream. At those moments, I often think: This is a dream, a pure diversion of my will, and since I have unlimited power, I am going to bring forth a tiger.
passages.context: The premise of Dreamtigers: the childhood obsession persists into the dream world, and in lucid dreams Borges tries to exercise his will to summon what he most wanted as a child.

passages.text: Oh, incompetence! My dreams never seem to engender the creature I so hunger for. The tiger does appear, but it is all dried up, or it's flimsy-looking, or it has impure vagaries of shape or an unacceptable size, or it's altogether too ephemeral, or it looks more like a dog or bird than like a tiger.
passages.context: The imagination's essential gap: the desired image arrives corrupted, partial, degraded. Even in the space of unlimited will, the tiger escapes. Dreaming cannot master the real creature.

passages.text: I feared sometimes that they would begin to veer off from reality; other times, that I would see my face in them disfigured by strange misfortunes. Now I have just learned that she has gone insane, and that in her room all the mirrors are covered, because she sees my reflection in them—usurping her own.
passages.context: The mirror anxiety transmitted from self to other: Borges' childhood fear of mirrors propagates into another person's madness—his face replacing hers in her own reflection. Identity theft through the reflecting surface.

passages.text: Averroës, trying to imagine what a play is without ever having suspected what a theater is, was no more absurd than I, trying to imagine Averroës yet with no more material than a few snatches from Renan, Lane, and Asín Palacios.
passages.context: Borges identifies his own impossibility with Averroës' impossibility—both men trying to imagine what they have no framework to grasp. The story is about the structural limits of imagination itself.

passages.text: One evening, the Muslim merchants spoke of actors... masked ones suffered imprisonment, but no one could see the jail; they rode upon horses, but the horse was not to be seen; they were killed, and then stood up again.
passages.context: Theater described to a man who has no concept of theater—the description is perfectly accurate and completely opaque. The gap between description and understanding is the gap between all worlds.

passages.text: He who has seen the universe, he who has beheld the fiery designs of the universe, cannot think in terms of one man, of that man's trivial fortunes or misfortunes, though he himself be that man. That man has been he; now he matters no more to him.
passages.context: The aftermath of total vision: the personal self becomes irrelevant. One who has seen everything can no longer care about the concerns of a particular life—including their own.

passages.text: And suddenly he disappeared, as though annihilated by a fire without light, and that with him disappeared the house and the unseen fountain and the books and the manuscripts and the pigeons and the many dark-skinned servant girls and the trembling red-haired slave girl and Farach and the rosebushes.
passages.context: The story ends with Averroës vanishing the moment Borges loses the ability to imagine him—the character's existence depends entirely on the author's imaginative act. Stop imagining, and the world unmakes itself.

passages.text: Time forks perpetually toward innumerable futures. In one of them I am your enemy.
passages.context: The statement that condenses the entire theory of the forking-path universe: every moment splits into multiple actual futures, all of which exist, in some of which we are enemies, in others friends.
